task: My dashboard polls the gateway with `{ id, method: "runs.get", params: { runId } }` and it works, but a CI lint flags `runs.get` as a legacy method name. What is the canonical v1 RPC method name I should switch to, and what scope does a token need to call it?

I'm minting a gateway token whose holder must list AND create/delete cron schedules and must list AND create/update work-doc tickets, but nothing else. Which canonical gateway scope strings do I put in the token's `scopes` array to cover exactly those four capabilities?

A slow browser tab subscribed to `streamRunEvents` keeps lagging and the gateway eventually tears its stream down. Which stable error code does the gateway return for an over-capacity subscriber that exceeded the bounded outbound queue, and what HTTP status maps to it?

Two on-call operators both POST `submitApproval` for the same paused gate within a second of each other. The first wins; what stable v1 error code and HTTP status does the gateway return to the second (loser) call?

When a client connects and reads the `features` array in the gateway's `hello` payload, which two feature strings does a `new Gateway({})` advertise by default (i.e. the default value of `GatewayOptions.features`)?

I launch a run over a single WebSocket connection and want its lifecycle events pushed to me without sending a separate subscribe frame. Which RPC methods automatically subscribe the calling connection to the affected run's events?

I registered a gateway extension namespace with resources, actions, and streams. What RPC method-name prefix routes resource (read) and action (write) calls, versus the prefix that routes stream (subscribe) calls?

My long-lived bot keeps dropping its WebSocket and missing run events. Which `SmithersGatewayClient` method gives me resilient run-event streaming that reconnects with backoff and resumes from the last observed seq, and which server event signals it should resume from a gap rather than re-fetch?
I want a local CLI process to reach a gateway that is listening on a unix domain socket (no TCP port). Which URL scheme does `SmithersGatewayClient` understand for a unix-socket WebSocket connection?

I'm building a custom workflow UI with `smithers-orchestrator/gateway-react` and want a single run's live state with no stale-data flashes. Which context provider component must wrap my tree, and which hook gives me the live run record by runId?
In a gateway-react operator console I need a live list of pending approvals and a live list of registered cron schedules. Which two gateway-react hooks provide those, respectively?

My app registered a gateway extension namespace exposing a resource, an action, and a stream. Name the three gateway-react hooks I use from the UI to read the resource, invoke the action, and subscribe to the stream.

A custom gateway-hosted UI needs to know its `rpcPath`/`wsPath`/`mountPath` at boot without a fetch. Which global variable does the gateway inject into the served HTML to bootstrap the client, and what is the TypeScript type of its value?

My agent calls `createTicket`, `updateTicket`, and `deleteTicket` (soft-delete) over the gateway, and separately `listTickets`. Which scope authorizes the three mutating ticket RPCs, and which scope authorizes listing tickets?

I want a read-only observability token that can call `listMemoryFacts`, `listPrompts`, `listScores`, and `listAccounts` over the gateway. Give the exact required scope string for each of those four RPCs (in that order).
For the DevTools Output tab I need both a single task iteration's produced output row and that node's diff over the gateway. Name the two RPC methods and the single scope that authorizes both.

An operator console needs to list pending approvals across runs, list the registered workflows, and list work docs over the gateway. Name those three RPC methods and the single scope all of them require.
I want a typed programmatic client (not React) to drive the gateway over HTTP RPC and WebSocket from a Node/Bun script. Which class do I import, and from which package subpath?

When the gateway returns an error response frame (e.g. a `Forbidden` for a missing scope), what typed error class does `SmithersGatewayClient` throw so I can read the `code`/`requiredScope`, and from which package does it come?

My time-travel debugger UI subscribes to the live DevTools component tree (snapshot + deltas) over the gateway. Which RPC method does it call, and which scope (not a run scope) does that method require?

An agent wants to rewind a stuck run to an earlier frame through the gateway. It found a legacy `jumpToFrame` method but isn't sure of the canonical name or what authorization is required. What is the canonical v1 RPC, what does its request body require, and exactly what authorization must the caller hold?
notes: A correct answer must name the canonical v1 method `rewindRun` (legacy aliases `jumpToFrame` / `devtools.jumpToFrame` route to it). The request requires `confirm: true` (literal) and a `frameNo` in [0, latestFrameNo]. Authorization is per-request: the connection needs `run:admin` scope AND must additionally be the run owner (connection `userId` === run `ownerId`) OR have `role: "admin"` â€” scope alone never grants it. Bonus: note it is destructive (truncates frames/attempts/output rows, reverts JJ sandboxes, emits `run.time_travel_jumped`) and is quota-limited (default 10/run/caller/hour â†’ `RateLimited`). Must NOT claim plain `run:write` or `run:read` is sufficient, or that scope alone authorizes it.

task: We deployed the gateway in `trusted-proxy` auth mode behind our ingress and didn't set `defaultScopes` or `allowedOrigins`. A request arrives with NO `x-user-scopes` header. What scopes and role does that connection get, and what does the default `allowedOrigins: []` actually enforce? Is this configuration safe?
notes: A correct answer must call out the footgun: in `trusted-proxy` mode a missing scopes header falls back to `defaultScopes`, which DEFAULTS TO `["*"]` (ALL scopes); a missing role falls back to `defaultRole` then `"operator"`. `allowedOrigins: []` means NO Origin allowlist is enforced (it allows all origins â€” it does NOT deny all). So an unconfigured trusted-proxy gateway grants full access to any request whose identity headers are absent, which is only safe behind a proxy you fully control that strips and rewrites the trusted identity headers (e.g. Cloudflare Access / internal API gateway). A correct answer recommends setting explicit `defaultScopes` and `allowedOrigins`. Must NOT claim missing scopes means no/empty scopes, or that `[]` origins denies all.

task: I'm configuring the gateway with `auth: { mode: "jwt", ... }` and my IdP issues HS256 tokens. Which JWT claims does the gateway read by default for scopes, role, and user id (and how do I override the claim names)? What algorithm and standard claims does it validate, and what happens when the scope claim is missing?
notes: Correct: by default JWT mode reads scopes from the `scope` claim, role from the `role` claim, and user id from the `sub` claim; these are overridable via `scopesClaim` / `roleClaim` / `userClaim`. It validates `alg=HS256` (HMAC) and checks `iss`, `aud`, `exp`, `nbf` (with `clockSkewSeconds` default 60). Scope claims may be arrays or space/comma-separated strings. A missing role falls back to `defaultRole` then `"operator"`; a missing scope claim falls back to `defaultScopes` then `[]` (NOT `["*"]` â€” unlike trusted-proxy mode). Must NOT claim it reads RS256/asymmetric keys by default or that missing scopes grant `*`.

task: I'm hand-writing a raw WebSocket client against the gateway instead of using the SDK. Walk me through the connect handshake: what frame does the server push first, what request must my client send to negotiate the protocol and authenticate, what does the server return, and how do I avoid an initial REST fetch for current state?
notes: A correct answer must describe: (1) on connect the server immediately PUSHES a `connect.challenge` event frame `{ nonce, ts }`; (2) the client replies with a `connect` REQUEST frame carrying `minProtocol`, `maxProtocol`, `client` metadata, `auth: { token }`, and optionally `subscribe: string[]` to filter events by runId; (3) the server returns a `hello` payload with `protocol`, `features`, `policy.heartbeatMs`, `auth` (`sessionToken`/`role`/`scopes`/`userId`), and a `snapshot`. The `snapshot` in `hello` is the initial-state snapshot that avoids an extra fetch. Bonus: after connect the gateway emits `tick` events every `heartbeatMs`. Must NOT invent a separate HTTP login endpoint or claim auth is sent as a header on the WS upgrade only.

task: On a single gateway WebSocket I see three kinds of JSON messages. What are the three frame types, which direction does each travel, how are responses correlated to requests, and what is the difference between the `seq` and `stateVersion` fields on a pushed event?
notes: Correct: the three frame types are `req` (`{ type: "req", id, method, params? }`, clientâ†’server), `res` (`{ type: "res", id, ok, payload?, error? }`, serverâ†’client, correlated to the request by matching `id`), and `event` (`{ type: "event", event, payload?, seq, stateVersion }`, server-pushed). On an event, `seq` is per-connection (monotonic within that socket, used for resume/gap detection) while `stateVersion` is global across the gateway. Must NOT claim JSON-RPC 2.0 `jsonrpc`/`result` fields or that `seq` is global.

task: How does the gateway keep idle WebSocket connections alive, what is the default interval, where does a client learn that interval at runtime, and what event name does the keep-alive use?
notes: Correct: the gateway emits a `tick` event on a fixed heartbeat. The interval is `GatewayOptions.heartbeatMs`, default `15_000` ms (15 s). The client learns the effective value from `policy.heartbeatMs` in the `hello` payload returned by `connect`. Must NOT claim it uses raw WebSocket ping/pong control frames as the documented mechanism or a different default like 30s.

task: I register a workflow with a `webhook` config that has a `secret`. A GitHub-style provider POSTs to it. By default, which request header does the gateway read to verify the payload signature, what algorithm/encoding and signature prefix does it expect, and what HTTP status does an invalid signature get?
notes: Correct: by default the gateway verifies the `x-hub-signature-256` header. It computes HMAC-SHA256 of the raw request body using the configured `secret`, hex-encoded, with the default prefix `sha256=` (e.g. `sha256=<hexdigest>`); the header name and prefix are overridable via `signatureHeader` / `signaturePrefix`. An invalid/missing signature is rejected with HTTP `401` (message like "Webhook signature verification failed") and increments `smithers_gateway_webhooks_rejected_total{reason="invalid_signature"}`. Must NOT claim plaintext token comparison, a `403`, or a non-SHA256 default.

task: I registered `gateway.register("github", wf, { webhook: { secret, signal: { name, correlationIdPath, ... } } })`. What URL path receives the webhook, and how does the gateway decide whether an incoming (verified) webhook delivers a signal to an existing waiting run versus starting a brand-new run?
notes: Correct: webhooks for a registered workflow are POSTed to `/webhooks/<workflowKey>` (e.g. `/webhooks/github`). After signature verification, the gateway extracts the signal `name` plus the correlation id (via `correlationIdPath`)/runId from the body; if it matches a run currently WAITING on that signal/correlation (e.g. a `<WaitForEvent>`/`<Signal>`), it delivers the payload as a signal to that run. If no matching waiting run exists, it STARTS A NEW run of the registered workflow (with `triggeredBy` like `webhook:github`). Bonus: it can also use a `run` config (`inputPath`) to map the body into run input. Must NOT claim webhooks always start a new run or always require an existing run.

task: I call `gateway.register("nightly", wf, { schedule: "0 8 * * 1-5" })`. How does the gateway turn that into automatic runs: what cron row does it write, how often does it poll, and what does `ctx.auth` look like inside a run that the cron fired (vs one a user launched)?
notes: Correct: registering with `{ schedule }` writes/syncs a cron row keyed `gateway:<name>` (e.g. `gateway:nightly`). The gateway's poller checks due schedules on an interval between 1 s and 15 s, clamped from `heartbeatMs`. A cron-fired run gets `ctx.auth.role = "system"`, `ctx.auth.triggeredBy = "cron:gateway"`, and `ctx.auth.scopes = ["*"]` (distinct from a user-launched run, whose auth carries the triggering token's role/scopes/userId). Bonus: `cronCreate`/`cronDelete`/`cronRun` RPCs need `cron:write`; `cronList` needs `cron:read`. Must NOT claim it uses an external OS cron daemon or that cron runs inherit a user's scopes.

task: Instead of typed scopes, I put the string `"getRun"` directly in a token's `scopes` array (a method-name grant). What can that token call? And if I grant `"runs.*"`, can that token reach a `run:admin` method like `hijackRun` just because the name matched? Explain the gating rule.
notes: Correct: a method-name grant (e.g. `"getRun"`) or a wildcard-prefix grant (e.g. `"cron.*"`/`"runs.*"`) authorizes invoking exactly the matched method(s), but it is GATED on that method's OWN required scope â€” it never escalates beyond what the method legitimately needs. So `"getRun"` lets you call `getRun` (a `run:read` method) and nothing more; a name/prefix grant can confer at most the scope the dispatched method itself requires. Therefore `"runs.*"` does NOT grant `hijackRun`/`rewindRun` privileges beyond their `run:admin` requirement just because the name matched â€” the grant only satisfies a method whose required scope it implies. `*` grants everything; legacy ranked grants (`read`/`execute`/`approve`/`admin`) still work for old tokens. Must NOT claim a name/prefix grant escalates to admin methods.

task: I'm hardening a public gateway against slow-client (slowloris) and oversized-body attacks. Which `GatewayOptions` bound slow header and body uploads (and their defaults), what is the default max POST body size for `/rpc`, and what error/status does an oversized response trigger?
notes: Correct: `headersTimeout` (default `30_000` ms) and `requestTimeout` (default `60_000` ms) are applied to the underlying Node HTTP server to bound slow header/body uploads â€” keep them below the reverse proxy's idle/read timeouts so Smithers closes slow clients first. `maxBodyBytes` defaults to `1_048_576` (1 MB) for `POST /rpc` and `maxPayload` defaults to `1_048_576` for WebSocket frames. An over-limit response surfaces the `PayloadTooLarge` error code mapped to HTTP `413`. Must NOT invent flags like `slowlorisTimeout` or claim there is no body cap.

task: Where does the gateway's OpenAPI spec come from, and what single source of truth do the client TypeScript types, the per-method scopes, the JSON schemas, and the OpenAPI document all derive from? Which package holds it?
notes: Correct: the contract package `@smithers-orchestrator/gateway` (exported subpath `./rpc`) holds `GATEWAY_RPC_DEFINITIONS` â€” the canonical array of per-method definitions, each with `method`, `requiredScope`, `transport`, inline JSON `requestSchema`/`responseSchema`, error list, and examples. This single registry is the source of truth: the OpenAPI document is generated from it (e.g. `packages/gateway/scripts/generate-openapi.ts` â†’ `packages/gateway/openapi.yaml`), and the SDK types and scope map derive from the same definitions. The error registry (`GATEWAY_RPC_ERRORS`) and scope vocabulary (`GATEWAY_SCOPE_VALUES`) live alongside it. Must NOT claim the OpenAPI is hand-written and the types are separately maintained.

task: I'm writing a gateway extension (`GatewayExtensions`) that exposes a chatty stream. What is the max payload size per extension frame, what happens to a subscriber that can't keep up, how does a long-running resource/action or stream handler learn the client went away, and what happens if I register the same namespace twice?
notes: Correct: extension payloads are bounded by `EXTENSION_PAYLOAD_MAX_BYTES = 4 * 1024 * 1024` (4 MiB) per frame. A subscriber whose per-stream outbound queue overflows (or whose ws.bufferedAmount exceeds the high-water mark) is disconnected with the `BackpressureDisconnect` code and its stream is torn down (slow-consumer protection, same spirit as DevTools). Handlers receive an `AbortSignal` â€” when the client unsubscribes or the connection closes, the signal aborts so resource/action and stream `subscribe` handlers should stop work (pending-handler abort). Namespace ownership is one-shot per registry: re-registering an already-registered namespace throws (collisions caught at register time). Resources/actions route via `ext.<namespace>.<key>`, streams via `ext.stream.<namespace>.<key>`; resource scope defaults to the namespace `defaultScope` then `run:read`, action then `run:write`. Must NOT invent unbounded payloads or claim re-registration silently overwrites.

task: I built a custom React UI for my `deploy` workflow with gateway-react. Where do I put the entry file by convention so the gateway hosts it, at what URL path does it mount, how do I attach it to a registered workflow, and what bundles it?
notes: Correct: by convention a workflow's custom UI entry lives at `.smithers/ui/<workflowKey>.tsx` (here `.smithers/ui/deploy.tsx`). The gateway mounts a workflow UI at `/workflows/<workflowKey>` (the gateway-level UI default path is `/`). You attach it either by the file convention or explicitly via the `ui` option to `gateway.register(name, wf, { ui: { entry, path?, title?, props? } })` (or `GatewayOptions.ui`). The entry is bundled server-side with Bun and served as HTML with the `__SMITHERS_GATEWAY_UI__` boot config injected. The React surface is `smithers-orchestrator/gateway-react` (Provider + hooks). Must NOT invent a `pages/` or `public/` convention or claim it must be pre-built by the user.

task: For cloud sync we put an ElectricSQL shape proxy in front of Smithers. How are a client's allowed shapes derived from its gateway identity, which HTTP endpoint serves shape reads, and what stops one tenant from reading another's rows?
notes: Correct: `@smithers-orchestrator/electric-proxy` is the scoped ElectricSQL shape proxy used for cloud sync (`createSmithersElectricProxy` / `serveSmithersElectricProxy`). Shape access is scoped by gateway grants: each shape in the catalog has a `requiredScope`, and the proxy authorizes a request against the caller's derived gateway scopes (missing scope â†’ HTTP `403` `missing required gateway scope`; unauthenticated â†’ `401`). Shape READS are served at `GET /v1/shape` (only `GET`; non-GET â†’ `405`); writes are committed back and observed as `electric.write.commit`/`electric.write.rejected` spans. It also enforces shape-open rate limits and a max-active-shapes cap (429) and strips/forwards auth. Scoping per shape + per-tenant scope derivation is what isolates tenants. Bonus: note PGlite cannot be an Electric source. Must NOT claim Electric talks to the Smithers DB directly with no proxy scoping.

task: A gateway-react app with client-side SQLite persistence calls `getSchemaSignature` on connect. What does this RPC actually return, why does a persistent client need it, and what scope does it require? (Hint: it is NOT a workflow's input/output schema.)
notes: Correct: `getSchemaSignature` returns the server's Smithers schema migration head plus a table-catalog signature used by client-side persistence â€” i.e. it lets a persistent client (SQLite-WASM/OPFS) detect when the server DB schema/migration version has changed so it can invalidate or rebuild its local store rather than serve stale/incompatible cached rows. It requires `run:read`. Must NOT describe it as returning a workflow's Zod input/output schema or a per-run schema.

task: A token has ONLY `run:write`. Over the gateway, can it call `getRun` (a `run:read` method) and `cancelRun`? Can it call `hijackRun` or `rewindRun`? Explain the scope-implication rule for the run/cron/ticket families.
notes: Correct: scopes are ranked within a family and a higher scope implies the lower ones. Run order is `run:read` < `run:write` < `run:admin`; cron is `cron:read` < `cron:write`; ticket is `ticket:read` < `ticket:write`. So a `run:write` token CAN call `getRun` (because `run:write` implies `run:read`) and `cancelRun`/`resumeRun`/`launchRun`, but CANNOT call `hijackRun` or `rewindRun` because those require `run:admin` (and rewind additionally requires run-owner/admin identity). Implication is only within a family â€” `run:write` does not grant `cron:*` or `ticket:*`. Must NOT claim `run:write` covers admin methods or that scopes are unranked.

task: I'm confused about the three HTTP-ish surfaces: `bunx smithers-orchestrator up --serve` (serve mode), `startServer()`, and `Gateway`/`smithers gateway`. When should I reach for the Gateway specifically, and what does it give long-lived clients that the other two don't?
notes: Correct: serve mode (`up --serve` / `createServeApp()`) is a single-workflow, single-run Hono HTTP surface for dev/single-purpose services. `startServer()` is a multi-workflow Node HTTP server with REST run lifecycle + SSE + approvals (production API gateway style, bearer-only, no scopes). `Gateway` is the headless control plane to reach for when long-lived clients (bots, dashboards, schedulers, custom UIs) need to authenticate once and stream events over WebSocket with resilient reconnection/resume, scoped tokens (run/approval/signal/cron/ticket scopes + JWT/trusted-proxy), pushed updates with a stale-data-free snapshot model, cron scheduling, and metrics across MANY registered workflows. Custom UIs (vanilla or React hooks) rely on the Gateway. Must NOT conflate serve mode with the Gateway or claim startServer supports scoped tokens/WebSocket subscriptions.

task: Can I call every gateway RPC over stateless HTTP `POST /rpc`, or are some methods WebSocket-only? Which ones can't be done over plain HTTP, and why?
notes: Correct: most RPCs are `http+websocket` (e.g. `launchRun`, `getRun`, `submitApproval`, `submitSignal`, `cronCreate`, `getNodeOutput` â€” callable via `POST /rpc` or `/v1/rpc` with the same `{ id, method, params }` body). The streaming subscriptions are WebSocket-only: `streamRunEvents` and `streamDevTools` have transport `websocket` because they push an open-ended series of event frames that a single HTTP response can't model (the HTTP/SSE equivalents live on the REST server, e.g. `GET /v1/runs/:id/events`). Auth on `POST /rpc` is via `Authorization: Bearer`/`x-smithers-key` (or trusted-proxy headers). Must NOT claim streamRunEvents/streamDevTools work over a single `POST /rpc` call.

task: My gateway is one process but the run was started detached by `smithers up -d` in a different process. How does the gateway still stream that run's live events to my WebSocket subscriber, and which option controls it?
notes: Correct: the gateway uses an out-of-process event bridge â€” `GatewayOptions.outOfProcessEventBridge` (default `true`) makes it poll the shared DB for newly persisted events from runs started by other processes and re-emit them to subscribers, so detached/out-of-process runs still stream live. The poll cadence is `outOfProcessEventBridgePollMs` (default `1_000`). This is why a single gateway can serve runs it didn't itself launch. Must NOT claim it requires the run to be in the same process or that it uses an in-memory-only event bus.

task: An old client calls `frames.list`, `frames.get`, `attempts.list`, `attempts.get`, and `runs.diff` on the gateway. Are these canonical v1 methods? Do they still work, what scope do they need, and what should a new client use instead for node output/diffs and the live tree?
notes: Correct: `frames.list`/`frames.get`/`attempts.list`/`attempts.get`/`runs.diff` are LEGACY method names, not part of the canonical v1 `GATEWAY_RPC_DEFINITIONS`. They are still accepted for backward compatibility and all require `run:read`. New clients should use the v1 methods: `getNodeOutput` and `getNodeDiff` for a node's output/diff, `streamDevTools` (the snapshot+delta tree, `observability:read`) for the live render tree, and `streamRunEvents` for run events. Must NOT claim these legacy names are removed/rejected or that they need elevated scopes.

task: A finished run did the right thing and I want to run the SAME workflow again with the exact input it was started with, without re-typing or re-fetching that input. Is there a gateway method for re-running from a run's stored input, and what scope does it need?
notes: Correct: the gateway accepts the legacy method `runs.rerun`, which starts a fresh run of the same workflow reusing the original run's stored input (rerun-from-stored-input). It requires `run:write` (same as launching). A correct answer points to `runs.rerun` (and notes that, equivalently, you can read the stored input from the run record and call `launchRun` with it). Must NOT claim there is no way to reuse stored input or that rerun needs `run:admin`.

task: When I start a `new Gateway({})` with no UI config, is there a built-in web console, at what path, and how do I turn it off? Is that console open to anyone who can reach the port?
notes: Correct: the gateway ships a built-in operator console enabled by default at path `/console` (`GatewayOptions.operatorUi` defaults to `{ path: "/console" }`). Set `operatorUi: false` to disable it. It is not open to anyone: gateway UI requests are authorized through the same auth pipeline (the boot/UI request flows through auth verification and the UI operates with the connection's role/scopes), so what an operator can see/do is gated by their token's scopes. Must NOT claim the console is unauthenticated by default or that there is no built-in console.

task: Write a minimal Smithers workflow that pauses until an inbound GitHub webhook (delivered by the gateway as a signal named `github.comment.created`, correlated by issue id) arrives, then records the event payload to a typed output. The workflow will be registered with `gateway.register("github", wf, { webhook: { secret, signal: { name: "github.comment.created", correlationIdPath: "issue.id" } } })`. Use the real waiting component â€” do not invent a `<Webhook>` tag.

Write a Smithers deploy workflow with one compute <Task> that builds an artifact, followed by an approval gate the GATEWAY will only let specific people decide: restrict the decision to callers who hold the `approval:submit` scope AND are in an allowed-users list, using the approval component's `allowedScopes`/`allowedUsers` props (the gateway enforces these before accepting `submitApproval`). Don't invent a `<Gate>` or `<RequireScope>` component.
canonicalAnswer: <Workflow name="deploy"><Task id="build" output={outputs.artifact}>{() => ({ ok: true })}</Task><Approval id="ship" allowedScopes={["approval:submit"]} allowedUsers={["alice","bob"]} /></Workflow>

task: Author one cohesive Smithers workflow intended to be registered on a Gateway (webhook + schedule) that models a release pipeline: run the steps in order with a <Sequence>; first <WaitForEvent> for a webhook-delivered signal `release.requested` (correlated by tag); then a compute <Task> that assembles the release; then a gateway-enforced scoped <Approval> (restricted via `allowedScopes`/`allowedUsers`) for a human to sign off; and a <Timer> that gives the approver a bounded window before the run auto-escalates. Wire typed outputs. Use only real components â€” no `<Webhook>`/`<Cron>` tags.
canonicalAnswer: <Workflow name="release"><Sequence><WaitForEvent id="req" event="release.requested" correlationId={ctx.input.tag} output={outputs.req} /><Task id="assemble" output={outputs.bundle}>{() => ({ ready: true })}</Task><Timer id="window" durationMs={3600000} /><Approval id="signoff" allowedScopes={["approval:submit"]} allowedUsers={["release-captain"]} /></Sequence></Workflow>
